sentences.text: The son wrote to his mother, 'I have been bad, and I have stolen.
And so I am now here in prison.
Go to gospozha Schohl-- her husband, [GERMAN] Dr.
Schohl, is here with me in prison.
And he said to tell his family that he is well, and he sends his love.' I mean, it was black on white, we couldn't understand.
And then we got the map out-- and we said, Zagreb, Tyrolia, it's on the way to the east-- it had to be.
We never-- that was the last we ever heard from our father.
That was the last we actually knew he was there.
Everything else that comes is guesswork.
The following year, August 1944, a gestapo sergeant came and said, Mother, come.
When Mother came back, he was with her.
He said, children, our father is dead.
He died in concentration camp Auschwitz, December 9th of last year.
What she told us was, when she went in there, and the gestapo told her that you are being sent back to Germany.
And my mother said to join my husband?
Surely my husband will be working for Germany.
Oh, didn't anybody tell you?
No.
He took a little piece of paper out of the drawer and said, Max Schohl, Jew number so-and-so, died of a heart attack, December 9, 1943, concentration camp Auschwitz.
So we never knew-- who are we going to believe?
Oh, my mother said, may I have this?
No.
And he put it back.
We had nothing.
We had trouble after the war to prove that our father was dead.
To this day, we have nothing to prove he is dead.
We tried and tried and tried.
Well, we never found out.
We tend to believe the general-- that it makes us feel better to think that he never entered the camp.
We had Serbian friends, and the lady who lived with us had a big garden.
And we lived mostly on fruits and vegetables from the garden.
No.
We never saw them again.
No.
No.
Oh, shootings almost every day.
We did not go back to the marketplace.
We did not see them but through the grapevine, we heard that there was another family alive, beside us.
Their Serbian landlord had a root cellar, and in 1941, when they rounded up all the Jews of Ruma, it was-- Lily Karolyi was a famous actress on the Burgtheater in Vienna.
Her brother was a journalist, and he was married to a German woman-- not Jewish.
That Serbian friend, landlord, made them a little room in the root cellar and fed them.
And at night, they came up and got air and walked around.
And they survived all these years, but we never saw them.
But we understood that his mind was not so good.
But they were alive and went back to Vienna.
That's what we heard.
In Ruma?
Exactly a year.
We were arrested and sent back to Germany.
We didn't ask why.
A gestapo sergeant.
Oh, yes I remember.
He came and took my mother across the street to the gestapo.
And he came back with my mother, and she said, pack only what you can carry-- pack warm things.
We're going back to Germany.
We didn't dare open our mouth.
He was there, watched us pack, and then he took the keys.
And unfortunately, the lady was not there.
But the four of us-- he walked behind us.
The three of us walked to the station.
And on the way to the station, you know, people waved to us-- going on a trip?
Mm-hm, going on a trip.
Of course, after we were gone, everybody knew what happened.
We sat-- it was so hot.
It was such a hot day.
And we didn't dare ask him anything.
Surely everybody who saw us must have seen that we were arrested.
We sat on our suitcases at the station.
And he stood in the shade and we waited.
All of a sudden, a cattle car came.
We were like, oh, my God.
And I don't know if you've ever seen pictures of cattle cars with these tiny windows, and those arms sticking out.
And the train stopped.
And my sister said, they're not going to get me on the train.
I'm going to kill myself before then.
And we looked, and he just stood there.
And my mother said, let's just-- don't do anything, let's just wait.
But right in front of us-- they must have come from Italy, because signora, signora-- they wanted water.
And finally my mother couldn't stand it anymore, and she turned around and she said to him, may I bring them water?
No!
I said, Mother, please, just-- just sit.
All of a sudden, the train-- you know, human beings are such selfish animals.
As the train started to roll, oh, thank God.
Such a sigh of relief, such selfishness that we're not on this train.
Isn't that horrible?
But that's the way we are.
Well, they didn't put us on the cattle train.
Now what?
I have no idea how long we sat there-- all of a sudden, a regular train came full of soldiers.
And all the soldiers had the uniform on from the Africa Corps.
They came from Africa-- Rommel had lost.
They were all going, heim ins reich, back home.
And he came and said, follow me.
Followed him, and he was looking and looking, and one of the cars had a little sign that said [GERMAN] one warden, three prisoners.
And he said, up.
We went up, and the soldiers went, ah, Fraulein, Fraulein.
And we looked at him, and he said [POINTING] And we walked in there, and that compartment was full of soldiers.
But on one side-- there were wooden benches-- on one side there was that sign again, one warden, three prisoners.
And we sat down.
We knew that was us.
And across from us were all these German soldiers.
And when we sat down, they burst out laughing, and laughed, and laughed, and said, leave it to our German women.
They will think of anything to get back home.
They thought we dreamt this-- because you see, all the Germans, or the German generation of Germans, knew what was happening, and they wanted to get back.
You couldn't.
The trains were soldiers-- that's all.
And they knew that.
And so they thought, our German women, how smart they are.
And this is their friend, and that's how they'll get.
And they talked to us like we were, you know-- the gestapo guy did not open his mouth.
No.
No.
The only rumors we heard in Ruma, from, through the grapevine, is that the British are coming from the south, and the Russians are coming from here, and Germany has lost the war.
No, jewelry she had.
Oh, sure, we lived on that for a long time.
We had nothing.
The German soldiers shared their food and their water with us.
And the guy didn't open his mouth.
Well, I know this was daytime, and I know we went through the night-- probably late the following afternoon.
When we went-- one of the soldiers said, we are now in Budapest.
The train stopped.
And a lot of them went out, and the Red Cross was at the station-- German Red Cross, naturally-- was at the station.
And they got on the train again.
And everybody, of course, was smoking in those days.
They brought us cigarettes.
And then we went through the countryside, and there was air raid and everybody out.
And my mother would say-- we were-- we were in the ditches with the rest of them.
The gestapo guy was right there laying with us, you know, in the ditches.
And we kept hoping they would bomb the train and see what happens then but they didn't.
We had to get back on the train.
He said to us-- no, my mother said to him, where are we?
And he said, in Vienna.
And she said, what are we going to do here?
He said, I have to take you to gestapo headquarters in Vienna, and then I'll have to say goodbye to you.
He was not one of the worst kind.
At least he left us alone.
We went-- it's unbelievable-- we went on a trolley, on a street car.
He knew we couldn't run away.
We had no papers.
We had nothing.
So he wasn't worried about us running away.
The four of us got on and got off and, oh, what a gorgeous palace-- that was the prison.
Went in there, and I cannot tell you how-- our cell-- oh, that's-- our cell [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] must have been once a gorgeous room.
But all the windows had been cemented over.
It was round.
It was like a cage, actually.
They made a, you know, a cage on one side, all the others walls, and three hard chairs in the middle.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] They took one by one.
We were never questioned together.
They took Mother first, and then they took one by one.
Day and night, the same questions.
Mother would put her lips to our ear and say, always say the same thing.
They want to know our contact with the partisans.
We know nothing.
And it was very difficult, you know, day after day.
No.
The only thing is, we were so thirsty-- we weren't hungry-- we begged for water.
We never got water.
And that makes you light-headed, and you sometimes don't know what you are saying.
And you sit.
My mother was a really-- showed us how to do it.
You can fall asleep sitting up straight and that's what we did.
No.
No.
No.
I have no idea.
I try to remember-- it couldn't have been more than days, maybe a week.
There were no sleeping.
We sat on the chairs and taught ourselves to sleep.
I cannot remember.
Three of us, yes.
You know, I don't remember that.
I have been trying to think about-- I know we had to go sometimes, but I can't remember.
This commandant called us one day together for the first time, the three of us, and he said, here are three pieces of paper that will get you on the train to Wiesbaden.
When you are in Wiesbaden, you will report to the Paulinenstrasse, which was the infamous gestapo street.
And go to the gestapo, and they will tell you where to go from there.
That was when we were-- that was when we were told we were to be separated.
My mother worked there, and we were to work-- it was a gestapo factory.
It was called [GERMAN].
And we had a room together with a warden-- a gestapo woman with a gold swastika.
She was very tall, black hair and-- [SIGHS]
When we saw somebody who had a gold swastika on, our heart fell in our shoes.
We were that afraid, because when Hitler first started, '22, 1923, he had maybe 100-- I don't know, it could be 500.
I don't know how many-- joined with him.
And when he came to power in '33, to thanks and in appreciation to his old cronies, he had these special swastikas made.
They had the gold band.
She wore it.
We never talked, with the exception of-- no, we-- we walked to work every morning, but she didn't even say anything like that.
I don't think we ever talked until the last day.
No.
She just watched us.
I mean--
We had one room.
There was a toilet in that room.
There was one bed.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the windows were, of course, boarded up.
There was no glass anyplace.
Our suitcases were there.
Oh, and a tin bowl and a bucket with water.
We left when it was still dark, and that was-- well, it was August, September.
And so it was early.
And we came home when it was dark, because it was fairly far to walk.
Mother usually was there when-- when we came.
We were trained as mechanics.
There were huge machines.
Some were printing machines, some were cutting machines.
The printing machines-- I was never on a printing machine, so I really don't know what was printed.
But the cutting machines were shoe soles-- insoles for the shoes.
Liberation was in April.
We got there in August of '44, and we were liberated April 26th, 1945.
She was strong.
She was always strong, although we knew she had a bad heart.
And she has, at that time, already had several little strokes.
No.
No.
We weren't allowed to talk.
Half Jews in one room.
That's where the young ones were.
And the other room were women and men.
And from what I learned, the Jewish part of the mixed marriage was there.
I've never seen it.
No.
I've never seen it.
We walked.
No.
Actually, they were indoor.
The only thing is, you had to use a bucket of water.
Nothing worked.
Our will to survive.
We must survive.
No.
Our Father said to us one time, never let them see you are afraid.
Never cry in front of them-- cry on the inside, never cry in front of them, and always hold your head up high.
Well, Kristallnacht was one, and the last time I saw my Father-- I knew it would have been the last time.
That was, yes.
There were [SIGHS] several.
The most astounding were the German soldiers, who, at one time brought us a goose when they saw what we had to eat.
Our Serbian friends, of course.
The Serbians used to say the Jews and the Serbians are brothers.
After the war-- I mean, after our liberation.
The 200-- now, I'm probably going to say that wrong.
They had a little axe on their sleeve, and they were called the Trail Blazers.
And I believe it was the 274th Infantry Regiment, but I could be mistaken, their number, here or there.
But they were the Trail Blazers.
Oh, that was tragic, really.
That was one of our sadder moments, our liberation.
We were sitting in the basement-- that thing was one of the very few times she-- she-- let us go in the basement.
And she sat next to us on the bench.
And all of a sudden, a little boy came downstairs and cried the Americans are here.
Looked at her.
She sat like a statue.
We didn't dare breathing.
I mean, what are we going to do?
And all of a sudden, she got up, never looked at us, and started to walk up the steps.
And I don't know why, but I saw something glittering-- it was the swastika.
Now, see this is a dumb thing.
I should have saved it.
I picked it up and it did me so good when I said, here, Frau so and so.
And she didn't want it.
She took it, never said a word.
We never saw her again.
We took over her room-- she with rooms-- we took those over.
And we sat, and finally Mother said, I guess we're going up.
We're free.
Oh.
No emotion, nothing.
You know, we used to dream that we would scream and laugh and cry and dance-- nothing.
Cold, total coldness on the inside.
Went upstairs, and Mother said, well, we may as well start cleaning up.
And I said, I want to wash myself.
And no, not yet, not yet.
But the first time we saw the American, there was a courtyard, and we took water and our dirty clothes and just-- and all of a sudden, stop!
Mm mm.
There was two American soldiers-- one tall, one not so tall-- filthy, dirty, with a drawn gun.
Never seen a more beautiful sight in my life.
I couldn't even make out the facial features.
But, oh, they were beautiful.
And I had made up a little speech.
Just stood there and looked at them.
And they said, guns, soldiers?
They were looking for soldiers.
And my mother said, for heaven's sake, tell them who we are.
And I said, we're Jews.
And they looked at each other and said, another one.
Everybody in Germany is Jewish or had a Jewish grandmother.
And that made me mad.
And when I get mad, oh, boy.
And I started to talk, and I said, we are Jews.
And the little one said, this is Lieutenant Michael Ruckman from Brooklyn.
And he's Jewish.
And I said, you are Jewish?
What's the holiest prayer, and what does a boy do when-- she's examining me.
Anyway, they were wonderful.
They brought us Jeep-loads of food.
And he is the one who-- I said to him one day-- he told us they were on their way to Japan.
But he said, when I leave, I will turn you all over to-- I said, I've got to earn money.
We don't have anything.
He's the one who got me-- who got this Major Walker, I think.
No, yeah, Walker, I think was his name.
That's how I became a hostess in an enlisted man's club, interpreter between the German waiters and the American company, whatever.
Well, of course, because every day one foot was in the grave.
You never knew, are you going to be alive tomorrow morning?
I mean, every day was your last day.
And that lasted a long time.
I was in Charleston.
I was married.
That's before I had children.
And we were riding in the car, and I said to my husband, there's a police car behind us.
And he said, don't you ever do that again.
So what?
I said, pull over.
Well, anyway, this is too long a story.
The fear was still in me.
And my husband, who was friendly with the Charleston police, had the guy come to the store so that I could talk to him.
I was shaking.
Do you still, today, think of the Shoah often?
Well, it's part of your life.
You dream, you-- you-- you-- you never-- you never forget it-- you never forget it.
We did for awhile.
But I'm so thankful for the life I have today.
All I do is thank God.
